order by mean lemurs_df1 <- lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(common_name) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  mutate(mean = round(mean, 1))

ggplot(lemurs_df1, aes(y = fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, mean)), x = mean)) +
  geom_point(lemurs_df, mapping = aes(x = age_at_death_y, y = common_name, color = sex)) +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df1, aes(x = mean, y = common_name), size = 6, color = "#5c4d7d") +
  ylab("Lemur Species") +
  xlab("Mean Age at Death") +
  ggtitle("Mean Age at Death for Lemur Species")+
  geom_text(aes(label=mean),size=2, color="white") +
  coord_cartesian(expand=F, clip="off") +
  labs(x="Lifespan (in years)", y="Lemur species",
       title="Lemur lifespan by taxonomy")

The code provided orders the lemurs dataframe (lemurs_df) by the mean age at death (age_at_death_y) for each lemurs species (common_name). It creates a new dataframe (lemurs_df1) that calculates the mean age at death for each lemurs species and rounds it to one decimal place.

It then uses ggplot to create a scatterplot with the y-axis representing the lemurs species (ordered in reverse) and the x-axis representing the mean age at death. The geom_point() function is used to add points to the plot, with the color representing the sex of the lemurs. The original lemurs_df is used for the points, while lemurs_df1 is used to add a larger point for the mean age at death for each species.

The plot also includes a y-axis label, x-axis label, plot title, and labels the mean age at death for each species. The coord_cartesian() function is used to remove padding around the plot and clip the points to the plot area. The labs() function is used to provide axis and title names for the plot.

Overall, the code organizes the data by mean age at death and provides a visual representation of the mean age at death for each lemurs species.
how to plot 2 quantitative and 1 categorical column

To plot 2 quantitative variables and 1 categorical variable, you can use a scatter plot or a line plot. Here's an example using ggplot2:

```R
# Assuming you have a dataframe named "data" with the variables you want to plot
ggplot(data, aes(x = quantitative_variable1, y = quantitative_variable2, color = categorical_variable)) +
  geom_point() +
  xlab("Variable 1") +
  ylab("Variable 2") +
  ggtitle("Scatter Plot of Two Quantitative and One Categorical Variable")
```

In this example, replace "data" with the name of your dataframe and "quantitative_variable1" and "quantitative_variable2" with the names of your two quantitative variables. Similarly, replace "categorical_variable" with the name of your categorical variable.

This code will create a scatter plot where the x-axis represents the first quantitative variable, the y-axis represents the second quantitative variable, and the color of the points represents the categories of the categorical variable. You can customize the plot further by adding labels, titles, changing point shapes, etc., according to your needs.
ggplot(lemurs_df1, aes(y = fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, mean)), x = mean))

The code `ggplot(lemurs_df1, aes(y = fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, mean)), x = mean))` is used to create a ggplot object with `lemurs_df1` as the data frame and `common_name` as the categorical variable on the y-axis and `mean` as the quantitative variable on the x-axis.

Here's a breakdown of the code:

- `ggplot(lemurs_df1, aes(...))`: This creates a ggplot object using `lemurs_df1` as the data frame and `aes()` to specify aesthetics or mappings. The `y = fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, mean))` in `aes()` indicates that the categorical variable `common_name` is mapped to the y-axis, reversing the order of the categories using `fct_rev()`, and reordering the categories based on the mean using `fct_reorder()`. The `x = mean` specifies that the quantitative variable `mean` is mapped to the x-axis.

This code segment sets up the initial plot with the categorical variable `common_name` on the y-axis in the specified order and the quantitative variable `mean` on the x-axis. However, since there is no geom or other layers added to this plot, it won't display any data or visual elements.

lemurs_df1 = lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(dlc_id) %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_at_death_y))

lemurs_df1 %>%
  group_by(common_name) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  mutate(mean = round(mean, 1)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(y=fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, desc(mean))), x=mean)) + 
  geom_point(aes(x= age_at_death_y, color=birth_type), shape=21) +
  geom_point(size=6, color="#5c4d7d")# +

The updated code filters out any rows where 'age_at_death_y' is NA and groups the resulting dataframe ('lemurs_df1') by 'common_name'. Then, it calculates the mean age at death for each 'common_name' group and rounds it to one decimal place.

Finally, it creates a ggplot object using the updated 'lemurs_df1' dataframe and specifies the aesthetic mappings. The 'fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, desc(mean)))' is used to reorder the categories on the y-axis based on the descending mean age at death. The 'mean' variable is mapped to the x-axis.

Here's the updated code:

```R
lemurs_df1 = lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(dlc_id) %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_at_death_y))

lemurs_df1 %>%
  group_by(common_name) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  mutate(mean = round(mean, 1)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(y=fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, desc(mean))), x=mean)) + 
  geom_point(aes(x=age_at_death_y, color=birth_type), shape=21) +
  geom_point(size=6, color="#5c4d7d")
```

This code will create a scatter plot. Each point represents a lemur species with the y-axis representing the lemur species (ordered based on the mean age at death), and the x-axis representing the mean age at death. The points are colored based on the 'birth_type' variable, and 'age_at_death_y' is used as the x-value for the points. The larger points are added to represent the mean age at death for each species.

```{r }

lemurs_df1 = lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(dlc_id) %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_at_death_y))

lemurs_df1 %>%
  group_by(common_name) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  mutate(mean = round(mean, 1)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(y=fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, desc(mean))), x=mean)) +
  geom_point(lemurs_df, aes(x= age_at_death_y, color=birth_type), shape=21) +
  geom_point(size=6, color="#5c4d7d") +
  scale_x_continuous(
  name = "Mean Age at Death",
  breaks = c(0, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30),
  labels = c("0", "10%", "20%", "20%")
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = "Lemur Species") +
  ggtitle("Mean Age at Death for Lemur Species")+
  geom_text(aes(label=mean),size=2, color="white")
```

The updated code includes additional modifications to the plot, such as specifying the breaks and labels on the x-axis using scale_x_continuous() and providing labels for the y-axis using scale_y_discrete(). It also adds a title to the plot using ggtitle() and labels the mean age at death for each species using geom_text().

Here's the updated code:

```R
lemurs_df1 = lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(dlc_id) %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_at_death_y))

lemurs_df1 %>%
  group_by(common_name) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  mutate(mean = round(mean, 1)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(y=fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, desc(mean))), x=mean)) +
  geom_point(lemurs_df, aes(x= age_at_death_y, color=birth_type), shape=21)+
  geom_point(size=6, color="#5c4d7d") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "Mean Age at Death",
    breaks = c(0, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30),
    labels = c("0", "10%", "20%", "20%")
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = "Lemur Species") +
  ggtitle("Mean Age at Death for Lemur Species")+
  geom_text(aes(label=mean),size=2, color="white")
```

This code will create a scatter plot with the mean age at death for each lemur species. The x-axis will have labels for "Mean Age at Death" and breaks at 0, 0.10, 0.20, and 0.30. The y-axis will display the lemur species names. The plot will have a title "Mean Age at Death for Lemur Species", and the mean values will be displayed as labels on the plot.

lemurs_df1 <- lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(dlc_id) %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  group_by(common_name) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  mutate(mean = round(mean, 1),
         label = sprintf('%.1f', mean))

lemurs_df1%>%
  ggplot(aes(y=fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, desc(mean))), x=mean)) + 
  geom_point(aes(x= age_at_death_y, color=birth_type), shape=21) +
  geom_point(size=6, color="#5c4d7d") +
  geom_text(aes(label=mean),size=2, color="white") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "Mean Age at Death") +
  scale_y_discrete(name = "Lemur Species") +
  ggtitle("Mean Age at Death for Lemur Species")

Here's the revised code with additional modifications:

```R
lemurs_df1 <- lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(dlc_id) %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  group_by(common_name) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  mutate(mean = round(mean, 1),
         label = sprintf('%.1f', mean))

lemurs_df1 %>%
  ggplot(aes(y=fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, desc(mean))), x=mean)) +
  geom_point(aes(x= age_at_death_y, color=birth_type), shape=21) +
  geom_point(size=6, color="#5c4d7d") +
  geom_text(aes(label=label), size=2, color="white") +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Mean Age at Death") +
  scale_y_discrete(name = "Lemur Species") +
  ggtitle("Mean Age at Death for Lemur Species")
```

In this updated code, there's a new step after calculating the mean age at death for each species called `mutate(label = sprintf('%.1f', mean))`. This creates a new variable called "label" that formats the mean age at death to be displayed as a label on the plot with one decimal place.

The `geom_text()` layer is modified to use the new "label" variable for displaying the mean age at death labels.

Overall, this code will create a scatter plot with the mean age at death for each lemur species. The points will be colored based on the "birth_type" variable. The x-axis will display the mean age at death, and the y-axis will display the lemur species names (reordered based on the descending mean age at death). The plot will also have a title.
save into new data frame summarise(mean = mean(age_at_death_y))# %>%

In order to save the summarized mean age at death into a new data frame, you can use the `summarize()` function and assign the result to a new variable. Here's an example:

```R
lemurs_mean <- lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(common_name) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(age_at_death_y))

head(lemurs_mean)
```

In this code, `lemurs_mean` is a new data frame that will contain the summarized mean age at death for each lemur species. The `summarize()` function is used to calculate the mean of the `age_at_death_y` variable within each `common_name` group. The result is then assigned to `lemurs_mean`. The `head()` function is used here to display the first few rows of the new data frame.

You can modify the code according to your specific requirements, such as adjusting the variable names or adding additional summarization calculations.
Error in geom_point(lemurs_df, mapping = aes(x = age_at_death_y), shape = 21) : ℹ Error occurred in the 1st layer. Caused by error in `desc()`: ! `x` must be a vector, not a function.

Apologies for the confusion. The error is due to using `lemurs_df` in the `geom_point()` function, which was incorrect. Instead, you should use `lemurs_df1` for the `geom_point()` function. Here's the corrected code:

```R
lemurs_df1 <- lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(dlc_id) %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  group_by(common_name) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  mutate(mean = round(mean, 1),
         label = sprintf('%.1f', mean))

lemurs_df1 %>%
  ggplot(aes(y=fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, desc(mean))), x=mean)) +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df, aes(x= age_at_death_y, color=birth_type), shape=21) +
  geom_point(size=6, color="#5c4d7d") +
  geom_text(aes(label=label), size=2, color="white") +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Mean Age at Death") +
  scale_y_discrete(name = "Lemur Species") +
  ggtitle("Mean Age at Death for Lemur Species")
```
In this code, `lemurs_df1` is the summarized dataframe that contains the mean age at death for each lemur species. The `geom_point()` function for the original `lemurs_df` is corrected by replacing it with `lemurs_df1`. The rest of the code remains the same as before.

Now the code should work correctly and create the desired scatter plot.

```{r}
 lemurs_df <- lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(dlc_id) %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  group_by(common_name)
  
lemurs_mean <- lemurs_df %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  mutate(mean = round(mean, 1))
lemurs_df1 %>%
  ggplot(aes(y=fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, desc(mean))), x=mean)) +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df, aes(x= age_at_death_y), shape=21) +
  geom_point(size=6, color="#5c4d7d?")
```

The code you provided has a few errors and inconsistencies. Here's a corrected version of the code:

```R
lemurs_df1 <- lemurs_df %>%
  group_by(dlc_id) %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  group_by(common_name) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(age_at_death_y)) %>%
  mutate(mean = round(mean, 1))

lemurs_df1 %>%
  ggplot(aes(y=fct_rev(fct_reorder(common_name, desc(mean))), x=mean)) +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df, aes(x= age_at_death_y), shape=21) +
  geom_point(data = lemurs_df1, size=6, color="#5c4d7d") +
  ggtitle("Mean Age at Death for Lemur Species") +
  geom_text(aes(label=mean), size=2, color="white") +
  coord_cartesian(expand=F, clip="off") +
  labs(x = "Mean Age at Death", y = "Lemur Species")
```

In this code, `lemurs_mean` is renamed to `lemurs_df1` to match the rest of the code. The `geom_point()` for `lemurs_df` is corrected to use `lemurs_df` instead of `lemurs_df1` since we want to plot the actual data points rather than the summarized mean points.

The `geom_point()` for `lemurs_df1` is modified to specify the data from `lemurs_df1`, the size of the points, and the color.

Additionally, I added some additional code to include a plot title, to label the mean age at death using `geom_text()`, and to adjust the plot coordinates and labeling using `coord_cartesian()` and `labs()` functions.

Please note that the `geom_point()` for `lemurs_df` assumes that the `geom_point()` for `lemurs_df1` was intended to be the same size as the points from `lemurs_df`. If you intended to use different sizes or shapes for these two sets of points, you can modify the code accordingly.
